meta.title: Early Math Foundations, Playful, Concrete Math for Ages 4-7 (K-2)
meta.description: Live online early math course for ages 4-7: counting with meaning, number bonds, shapes, measurement and playful problem solving, taught concrete-first in short, joyful, interactive classes. 8 classes a month, free demo first.
meta.level: Ages 4-7 (kindergarten to grade 2), all starting points
meta.commitment: 2 live one-hour classes per week (structured as playful segments) + everyday math play at home
meta.certification: Course completion certificate + stage mastery checks

program_overview.title: Early Math Foundations
program_overview.tagline: The years that decide whether math becomes a friend. We make it one.
program_overview.description: A 12-month live program for children aged 4-7, the years when a child's whole relationship with mathematics is quietly decided. Early math done right is not worksheets; it is counting that means something, quantities seen and handled, patterns spotted, shapes built and stories solved. Our mentors teach concrete-first in genuinely interactive classes structured as short playful segments that respect young attention spans: real objects, ten-frames, games and math talk, with the child doing and explaining rather than watching. Children leave this course fluent in early number, confident in their own thinking, and, the part parents notice most, actually liking math.
what_makes_this_different: Concrete before abstract, always: quantities are handled, drawn, and only then written as symbols
Classes built for young attention: an hour of varied segments, games, movement, building, story problems, never an hour of sitting still
Counting taught with meaning: one-to-one correspondence, cardinality and the ideas most programs skip straight past
Number bonds and part-whole thinking started early, gently, the foundation everything to age 18 stands on
Math talk from day one: even five-year-olds explain their thinking here, in their own words
One dedicated mentor who knows the child, their pace and their spark
Parents equipped too: simple everyday math-play prompts, because kitchens teach more than worksheets
The honest opposite of drill academies, no pressure, no rankings, no tears

learning_path.phase_1: Counting with meaning, comparing quantities, and the first part-whole ideas, through objects and games
learning_path.phase_2: Addition and subtraction as stories, bonds to 10, place value beginnings and the teen numbers demystified
learning_path.phase_3: Numbers to 100 and beyond, early measurement, shapes and data, plus problem-solving courage and grade-2 readiness

career_outcomes: A child who says 'I like math', the outcome that predicts the next decade
Number sense foundations that make grades 3-5 feel natural
Fine reasoning habits: explaining, checking, trying another way
A smooth on-ramp into Mental Maths Mastery or the Elementary Masterclass

phase_1_foundation.title: PHASE 1: Counting That Means Something (Months 1-4)
phase_1_foundation.description: Counting looks simple and hides deep ideas: one number per object, the last number telling the total, quantities compared and conserved. This phase builds those ideas properly through objects, movement and games, the difference between reciting numbers and understanding them.

month_1.title: Month 1: Numbers as Quantities
month_1.week_1: Playful diagnostic disguised as games: counting, comparing and pattern instincts observed
month_1.week_2: One-to-one counting with real objects; the cardinality idea, the last number IS the amount
month_1.week_3: Subitizing: seeing 3, 4 and 5 without counting, dice, dots and finger patterns
month_1.week_4: Comparing quantities: more, fewer, same, justified with matching, not guessing

month_2_3.title: Months 2-3: The Number World to 10
month_2_3.week_5_6: Numerals connected to quantities; counting forwards and backwards as a game
month_2_3.week_7_8: Ten-frames as home base: five-and-some structure, seeing numbers inside numbers
month_2_3.week_9_10: Part-whole play: 5 is 2-and-3, 4-and-1, decomposition as a toy, years before it is a technique
month_2_3.week_11_12: Ordinal numbers, position words and the number line met as a walking game

month_4.title: Month 4: Patterns and Sorting + Stage Checkpoint
month_4.week_13: Repeating patterns: copy, extend, create, the beginnings of algebraic thinking
month_4.week_14: Sorting and classifying by attributes; explaining the rule you sorted by
month_4.week_16: Stage mastery check through play: show it, say it, teach it to your bear

phase_2_operations.title: PHASE 2: Stories, Bonds and the Teen Numbers (Months 5-8)
phase_2_operations.description: Addition and subtraction arrive as stories acted out with objects, joining, separating, comparing, long before symbols carry them. Bonds to 10 grow toward automaticity through games, and the famously confusing teen numbers get demystified with place-value materials.

month_5.title: Month 5: Addition and Subtraction as Stories
month_5.week_17_18: Joining and separating stories acted with objects; the plus and minus symbols as story shorthand
month_5.week_19_20: Counting on (not starting over) and counting back; the empty-hand subtraction game

month_6.title: Month 6: Bonds of 10 and Fact Families
month_6.week_21_22: Bonds of 10 through every game we know, the single most valuable fact set in mathematics
month_6.week_23_24: Fact families: 3+7, 7+3, 10-7, 10-3 as one idea seen four ways

month_7.title: Month 7: Teen Numbers and Place Value Beginnings
month_7.week_25_26: Teens demystified: 14 as ten-and-four, built with materials, where place value actually starts
month_7.week_27_28: Numbers to 20: counting, comparing, and bridging-through-10 previewed as a game

month_8.title: Month 8: Story Problems and the Explain Habit
month_8.week_29_30: Word problems as theatre: act it, draw it, then write it, the three-step that prevents future word-problem fear
month_8.week_31_32: Missing-number puzzles and both-ways thinking; stage mastery check through play

phase_3_expansion.title: PHASE 3: To 100 and Into the World (Months 9-12)
phase_3_expansion.description: The number world expands to 100 with place-value understanding, and math steps into the child's world: measuring, money, time, shapes and simple data. The course closes with problem-solving courage and a confident handoff to grade-level mathematics.

month_9.title: Month 9: The Road to 100
month_9.week_33_34: Tens and ones with materials: building two-digit numbers, comparing and ordering them
month_9.week_35_36: Skip counting by 2s, 5s and 10s as rhythm and pattern; the 100-square explored like a map

month_10.title: Month 10: Two-Digit Confidence
month_10.week_37_38: Adding tens, adding ones: two-digit addition with materials first, mental jumps second
month_10.week_39_40: Ten-more ten-less fluency; friendly-number thinking previewed gently

month_11.title: Month 11: Measurement, Money and Time
month_11.week_41_42: Length, weight and capacity by comparing and then by counting units; estimation as a guessing game with feedback
month_11.week_43_44: Coins and simple shopping play; o'clock and half-past on real clocks

month_12.title: Month 12: Shapes, Data and Graduation
month_12.week_45_48: 2D and 3D shapes built, named and hunted at home; halves and quarters as fair sharing, fractions met kindly
month_12.week_49_52: Simple pictographs from the child's own surveys; graduation project, the child teaches their favourite math game, and the road-ahead plan into grade-level math

faqs.question: Can a 4-year-old really learn in online classes?
faqs.answer: Yes, with the right design, which is the whole craft here. Classes run as short, varied playful segments: a counting game, a building challenge, a story problem, a movement break with skip counting. The mentor works with real objects the child holds up to the camera, and parents of our youngest students sit nearby for the first weeks. The free demo shows you exactly how it works with your child.

faqs.question: Isn't this age too early for structured math?
faqs.answer: It is exactly the right age for the right kind: playful, concrete, talk-rich mathematics. Research on early number sense is unambiguous, the part-whole and counting ideas built at 4-7 predict mathematics success for years. What would be wrong at this age is worksheets and drilling, which is why we do neither.

faqs.question: How is this different from apps like Khan Kids or busy-work workbooks?
faqs.answer: Apps entertain and practice; they cannot listen. The engine of early math learning is math talk, a child explaining 'I saw 5 because 4-and-1' to a person who responds. Our mentors have that conversation twice a week, notice exactly which ideas have landed, and adjust in real time. No app does that.

faqs.question: My child already loves numbers. Will this stretch them?
faqs.answer: Happily, the mentor teaches to stage, not age. A number-loving five-year-old might race into part-whole play and early place value while a classmate consolidates counting. Small groups and 1-on-1 make that flexibility real.

faqs.question: What do parents need to do at home?
faqs.answer: Minutes, not hours: we send simple everyday prompts, count the stairs, share the strawberries fairly, spot the pattern on the tiles. Kitchens and car rides teach more early math than worksheets ever did, and we show you how to use them.

faqs.question: What comes after this course?
faqs.answer: Graduates flow into Mental Maths Mastery (for fluency depth) or the Elementary Mathematics Masterclass (the full grades 1-5 program) with the same mentor where possible, arriving with the foundation those courses quietly assume.

faqs.question: What does the course cost?
faqs.answer: Small-group classes are ₹1,499 per month and private 1-on-1 mentorship is ₹4,999 per month in India; international students pay USD $100 per month for group and $150 for 1-on-1. Both include 8 live classes a month, recordings, and a free demo class before any payment.

faqs.question: Can we try it before paying?
faqs.answer: Yes. Every child starts with a free live demo class that doubles as the playful diagnostic, no card details, no obligation, and you will know within the hour whether your child lights up.

testimonials.name: Sonam Oswal, mother of Dhairya (verified Google review)
testimonials.text: My child Dhairya is really enjoying the classes. This is his first online class, and he eagerly looks forward to it. I can see his improvement.

testimonials.name: Shewta Singh, mother of Ishan (verified Google review)
testimonials.text: My son struggled with math for years. Integrating it into coding projects has transformed his understanding and confidence. Highly recommended!

prerequisites.parent_role: Nearby for the youngest children in early weeks; cheering thereafter

who_is_this_for.k_2_students: Kindergarten to grade 2 children whose school math needs a stronger foundation underneath
who_is_this_for.eager_minds: Young children already fascinated by numbers who deserve real food for it
who_is_this_for.worried_starts: Children whose first school math experiences created tears, reversible at this age, quickly

career_paths_after_completion: Ready for Mental Maths Mastery with the bonds already forming

course_guarantees.live_classes: Live, interactive classes with a real instructor, never pre-recorded videos.
course_guarantees.small_batches: Small batches only: group classes are capped at 10 students, with mini-batch (3 to 4 students) and personal 1-on-1 options.
course_guarantees.structured_curriculum: A structured, well-paced curriculum taught step by step, with hands-on practice in every session.
course_guarantees.doubt_support: Doubt support between classes over WhatsApp, so you are never left stuck.
course_guarantees.certificate: A course-completion certificate you can share.
course_guarantees.free_demo: A free demo class before you enrol, so you can decide with no pressure.
